mechanism_narrative: Rising industrial production expands output and corporate profits, boosting investor sentiment and leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns over a 20-day horizon. The signal is always active because production trends directly reflect economic momentum.
failure_condition: The rolling 2-year rank correlation between INDPRO level and 20d forward SPX returns is less than 0.05 or negative, or the in-sample t-statistic of a univariate regression is below 1.96.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate exceeds 5.0%, it signals labor market weakness, declining consumer spending, and reduced corporate earnings, causing negative S&P 500 forward returns over the following 60 trading days.
failure_condition: In periods when UNRATE exceeds 5.0%, the average 60d forward return is not significantly negative (one-sided t-test p > 0.05) or the proportion of positive returns exceeds 50%.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous month-over-month increases in retail sales and industrial production indicate synchronized demand-supply expansion, confirming robust economic growth and leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns over a 20-day horizon. The joint signal is stronger than either series alone.
failure_condition: The interaction term (RSAFS_change * INDPRO_change) in a linear regression of 20d forward returns has a p-value > 0.10 or the sign is negative over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: In the low unemployment regime (UNRATE below 5%), rising retail sales reflect strong consumer demand that directly boosts corporate earnings, generating positive S&P 500 forward returns over a 20-day horizon. The effect is muted or absent when unemployment is high.
failure_condition: The predictive slope of RSAFS on 20d returns in the low-UNRATE regime is not significantly greater than in the high-UNRATE regime (Chow test p > 0.10), or the regime-specific Rank IC is below 0.03 over a 2-year window.

mechanism_narrative: A rising consumer price index erodes real purchasing power and signals impending monetary tightening, which compresses equity valuations and produces negative S&P 500 forward returns over a short 5-day horizon. The negative relationship is always active because inflation expectations adjust continuously.
failure_condition: The rolling 1-year correlation between CPIAUCSL level and 5d forward returns is positive or above -0.05, or the mean excess return after positive CPIAUCSL surprises is not negative.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate rises above 1.0%, it marks a shift from accommodative to tightening monetary policy, increasing discount rates and reducing equity valuations, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over a 60-day horizon.
failure_condition: During periods when FEDFUNDS exceeds 1.0%, the average 60d forward return is not significantly negative (one-sided t-test p > 0.05) or the hit rate of positive returns exceeds 40%.

mechanism_narrative: Concurrent monthly expansions in nonfarm payrolls and retail sales indicate a virtuous cycle of employment-driven consumption, boosting corporate earnings and driving positive S&P 500 forward returns over a 20-day horizon. The interaction amplifies the standalone predictive power of each series.
failure_condition: The interaction term (PAYEMS_change * RSAFS_change) in a linear regression of 20d forward returns is not statistically significant (p > 0.10) or has a negative coefficient over a 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: In an accommodative monetary regime (federal funds rate below 2.0%), rising industrial production has a particularly strong positive effect on S&P 500 forward returns over a 20-day horizon because cheap credit amplifies the impact of real output growth on earnings. The effect is weaker or negative when the central bank is tightening.
failure_condition: The predictive slope of INDPRO on 20d returns in the low-FEDFUNDS regime is not significantly greater than in the high-FEDFUNDS regime (Chow test p > 0.10), or the regime-specific Rank IC is below 0.03 over a 2-year window.
